title: The State of Simulation for Physical AI: An Overview
byline: Johnny Nuñez Cano, Mitesh Patel, Asier Arranz, lior ben horin, Raymond Lo
excerpt: A Blog post by NVIDIA on Hugging Face
content_html:

Why Simulation

Figure 1: Humanoid robot locomotion simulation. The robot's pose is represented by tracked body keypoints (green markers), while successive robot instances illustrate its movement through time. Directional arrows indicate commanded motion, demonstrating the use of a physics-based simulation environment for training and evaluating robot locomotion and control policies.

The primary challenge in building physical AI systems is data availability. Large language models (LLMs) and vision-language models (VLMs) can be trained on internet-scale datasets, but robotics and physical AI systems do not have the same advantage. To train a physical AI system, a robot must learn the consequences of interacting with the physical world. For example, it needs to understand what happens when a cup slips, a cable bends, or a gripper contacts an object at the wrong angle.

Collecting this kind of data in the real world is slow, expensive, risky, and sometimes impractical due to the destructive nature of the tasks. Simulation provides a bridge by enabling developers to generate large amounts of photorealistic, physically grounded data. By teleoperating robots in simulation and scaling data collection through GPU parallelism, developers can generate thousands of hours of robot experience at a fraction of the cost of real-world collection.

Earlier robotics simulators were often used primarily to debug geometry, test controllers, or visualize robot motion. Today, simulation has become part of the model development loop. Teams use it to generate perception datasets, train reinforcement learning policies, collect demonstrations, augment real-world data, benchmark models, and test policies against rare or adversarial scenarios. This shift is why industrial research labs and academic groups are increasingly contributing to, or developing, simulation engines that can meet these new requirements.

These requirements can be understood through a three-computer paradigm:

  • Training computer: A large GPU cluster used to process data and train foundation AI models.
  • Simulation computer: A GPU workstation or cluster using GPU-accelerated physics and RTX rendering to generate robot experience, sensor data, and simulated interactions.
  • On-robot computer: An edge device, such as a NVIDIA Jetson AGX Thor-class system, that runs the trained policy or model during deployment.

Figure 2: A physical system (Earth and robot) continuously exchanges data with its virtual representation (digital model), enabling monitoring, analysis, prediction, and control through a bidirectional feedback loop.

Each computer plays a different role depending on the task’s latency, throughput, accuracy, and deployment requirements.

Many simulation engines are available today, each targeting different use cases such as humanoid robots, aerial vehicles, ground robots, autonomous vehicles, quadrupeds, and dexterous manipulators. These engines also differ in their support for reinforcement learning, batched simulation, contact-rich physics, photorealistic rendering, and sensor simulation. Refer to Figure 2 for a categorization of simulation engines by robot domain and acceleration profile.

Which Simulation Engine Should I Use?

Developers often struggle with which simulation engine to use for the use case they are tackling. Oftentimes, this falls on a few fundamental questions, such as

  • Do I need scalable synthetic data generation workflows?
  • Do I need to do reinforcement learning?
  • What kind of sensor support does a given simulation engine have?
  • What kind of 3D asset formats does the simulation engine support?
  • What environmental fidelity and scale are required?

These factors would help developers in deciding which simulation engine to use for their development needs.

NVIDIA Isaac Sim and Isaac Lab, MuJoCo, PyBullet, Drake, and Genesis are some of the most popular simulation engines for humanoids and two-arm dexterous manipulation. Each of these simulation engines is unique to specific use cases such as scaling reinforcement learning, generating synthetic data, testing algorithms and/or models in simulation. Here we will provide a quick overview of MuJoCo, MuJoCo Warp, NVIDIA Isaac Sim, and Isaac Lab.

MuJoCo

MuJoCo, short for Multi-Joint dynamics with Contact, is a fast, accurate, open-source physics engine built mainly for robotics, biomechanics, reinforcement learning, control, and simulation of articulated systems. Unlike engines focused mostly on games or visual realism, MuJoCo is designed around precise dynamics, contact-rich motion, and model-based optimization. MuJoCo emphasizes speed, accuracy, and modeling power for systems where physical correctness matters.

Figure 3: Robotic manipulator simulated in MuJoCo. The figure illustrates a multi-joint robotic arm with a parallel gripper operating in a physics-based simulation environment. MuJoCo is used to model the robot's dynamics, kinematics, and contact interactions, enabling the development and evaluation of motion planning, manipulation, and control algorithms prior to real-world deployment.

While MuJoCo is not always the best choice for photorealistic rendering or massive GPU-parallel simulation, but some of the core features that MuJoCo is well known for are a deterministic pipeline, well-defined inverse dynamics with contacts, strong contact modeling, and generalized-coordinate simulation.

MuJoCo Warp

MuJoCo Warp, also called MJWarp, is a GPU-accelerated implementation of MuJoCo written in NVIDIA Warp, a Python framework for writing high-performance CUDA-accelerated differentiable kernels - tailored for robot learning and physics simulation.

It brings MuJoCo-style articulated-body physics, contact-rich simulation, and robot dynamics into a batched GPU setting, making it well suited for reinforcement learning and large-scale policy training. Compared with classic CPU MuJoCo, MJWarp is aimed more at throughput than single-step latency: it is designed to simulate many worlds in parallel, reduce CPU-GPU transfer bottlenecks, and scale contact-heavy robot tasks more efficiently for learning workloads.

Isaac Sim

Isaac Sim is an open-source robotics simulation framework built on NVIDIA Omniverse. It uses OpenUSD as its core scene and data layer, allowing robots, environments, sensors, materials, lighting, and physics properties to be represented as USD prims and schemas. Isaac Sim provides high-fidelity physics through PhysX, photorealistic RTX rendering, and robotics-focused sensor simulation for cameras, depth, lidar, radar, segmentation, and synthetic data generation workflows. It can ingest assets from CAD, URDF, MJCF, USD, and real-world reconstruction pipelines, enabling robots to be tested in realistic digital-twin environments.

Isaac Lab

NVIDIA Isaac Lab 3.0 is an open-source, GPU-accelerated, agent-ready simulation framework for robot learning, designed to train and evaluate robot policies at scale. This direction supports agent-assisted workflows for building environments, tuning physics, debugging, profiling, sim-to-sim transfer, and sim-to-real deployment. Its modular manager-based and direct workflows support reinforcement learning, imitation learning, motion planning, data collection, and policy evaluation.

How Isaac Lab 3.0 Relates to Isaac Sim and Newton

With Isaac Lab 3.0.0 - We have decoupled the Isaac Sim and omniverse dependency, and made Isaac Lab a lightweight, multi backend Robot learning framework.

The new architecture separates backend-specific code from the core Isaac Lab API. Developers can select to use Isaac Sim with PhysX and RTX rendering for photorealistic, sensor-rich workflows, or run light-weightheadless Newton physics for high-throughput simulation. Additionally, developers can add photoreal sensors via the standalone OVRTX renderer, or leverage the Newton renderer for vision-based RL tasks that require a very large number of environments.

Modern GPU-Accelerated Physics for Robotics

Modern robotics simulation frameworks are powered by numerical physics: rigid-body and soft-body dynamics, contact and friction models, joint constraints, actuators, sensors, and time integration. At each simulation step, the engine estimates how bodies move under forces, torques, collisions, gravity, constraints, and controller inputs. For robot learning, this physics must be fast, stable, repeatable, and scalable enough to run thousands of parallel environments, while also being accurate enough to transfer learned behavior from simulation to real robots. To address these, the Newton library is created.

Newton

Newton is an open-source, GPU-accelerated, extensible, and differentiable physics engine developed by NVIDIA, Google DeepMind, and Disney Research, and managed through the Linux Foundation. It is built on NVIDIA Warp and OpenUSD, and it integrates MuJoCo Warp as a key physics backend. In the broader simulation ecosystem, Newton acts as a modern physics layer for robot learning frameworks such as Isaac Lab and MuJoCo Playground, giving researchers a path toward scalable, differentiable, simulator-agnostic physics. In Isaac Lab specifically, Newton is currently an active integration path, with the MuJoCo-Warp solver used as a primary focus for high-throughput robot-learning experiments. Figure showcases breakdown on how different application frameworks communicate with GPU accelerated physics libraries and simulation engines. Newton provides multiple solver implementations rather than prescribing one numerical method for every problem. SolverMuJoCo and SolverFeatherstone use generalized coordinates for articulated rigid-body systems. SolverSemiImplicit, SolverXPBD, and the SolverKamino use maximal-coordinate formulations. SolverVBD is an implicit solver supporting rigid bodies, particles, cloth, and soft bodies, although its joint support remains limited. SolverImplicitMPM targets particle-based continuum materials, while SolverStyle3D specializes in cloth simulation. Because capabilities differ between solvers—including support for articulations, deformable bodies, contacts, and differentiation—the appropriate solver depends on the physical system being modeled.

Figure 4: The Newton Physics Engine is an open-source, GPU-accelerated simulation engine built upon NVIDIA Warp, designed for roboticists and simulation researchers.

Other Simulation Engines

PyBullet remains a useful CPU baseline for quick prototyping; DART and ODE are still Gazebo back-ends; Drake is the gold standard if you need contact-implicit trajectory optimisation and rigorous numerics rather than throughput. Each has a niche. None of them is the answer to “I need 4,096 humanoids on one GPU.”

Conclusion

Robot simulation has quietly become one of the most important layers in the modern embodied AI stack. As models move from static datasets into physical interaction, the ability to generate high-quality, scalable, and diverse simulated experiences is no longer optional, it is foundational. Simulation is where policies are stress-tested, where data is amplified, and increasingly, where entire training pipelines begin.

For a long time, the robot-simulator conversation has focused on "which engine performs the fastest" and for many workloads this statement still holds true. What we find more interesting in 2026 is a different question: which pieces of the stack are likely to become the shared infrastructure that different engines would build on top of? There is no definite answer out there yet but a few candidates seem plausible from where we sit. The ecosystem is fragmenting and recomposing into a layered stack, where different tools specialize, interoperate, and sometimes compete to define the abstractions others will build on.

What we do feel confident saying is that an unusual amount of this work is being done in the open-source, openly governed, and available to anyone with a consumer GPU. A growing share of the core infrastructure physics engines, differentiable simulators, and training pipelines is emerging in the open, with open-source projects, open governance, and GPU-accessible tooling lowering the barrier to entry and accelerating iteration. That alone is worth celebrating, regardless of which engines you end up reaching for.

Looking Ahead: From Concepts to Practice

This article takes a step back to map that landscape in 2026: what exists, how the major engines relate, and why these shifts matter for anyone building in Physical AI. The next article in this series goes one layer deeper and looks at Warp and MuJoCo Warp itself, what it is, and how to drive it. From there, we will work our way up, and by the end of the series we will have a practical end-to-end robotics example, trained on a GPU using a software stack that did not exist two years ago.

If you build something with the tools described here, whichever ones you pick, we would love to see it. Open an issue on any of the linked repositories, or reach out to us on Discord Nvidia Omniverse.

Further Learning Resources

  • Practical Implementation: If you want to go deeper alongside this series, you can follow NVIDIA’s SO-101 sim-to-real course, which walks through the full pipeline from simulation to physical deployment with Isaac Sim, Isaac Lab, and GR00T.
  • The Big Picture: For a broader view of the space, NVIDIA’s Physical AI learning paths provide structured guidance across simulation, robotics, and embodied AI workflows.

Teams use it to generate perception datasets, train reinforcement learning policies, collect demonstrations, augment real-world data, benchmark models, and test policies against rare or adversarial scenarios. This shift is why industrial research labs and academic groups are increasingly contributing to, or developing, simulation engines that can meet these new requirements.

blocks.html_en: NVIDIA Isaac Sim and Isaac Lab, MuJoCo, PyBullet, Drake, and Genesis are some of the most popular simulation engines for humanoids and two-arm dexterous manipulation. blocks.html_en: MuJoCo

sentences.en: MuJoCo
sentences.zh: 穆乔科

blocks.html_en: MuJoCo, short for Multi-Joint dynamics with Contact, is a fast, accurate, open-source physics engine built mainly for robotics, biomechanics, reinforcement learning, control, and simulation of articulated systems. Unlike engines focused mostly on games or visual realism, MuJoCo is designed around precise dynamics, contact-rich motion, and model-based optimization. MuJoCo emphasizes speed, accuracy, and modeling power for systems where physical correctness matters.

sentences.en: MuJoCo, short for Multi-Joint dynamics with Contact, is a fast, accurate, open-source physics engine built mainly for robotics, biomechanics, reinforcement learning, control, and simulation of articulated systems.
sentences.zh: MuJoCo 是“接触式多关节动力学”的缩写，是一种快速、准确、开源的物理引擎，主要用于机器人、生物力学、强化学习、控制和关节系统的仿真。

sentences.en: Unlike engines focused mostly on games or visual realism, MuJoCo is designed around precise dynamics, contact-rich motion, and model-based optimization.
sentences.zh: 与主要关注游戏或视觉真实感的引擎不同，MuJoCo 是围绕精确的动力学、丰富的接触运动和基于模型的优化而设计的。

sentences.en: MuJoCo emphasizes speed, accuracy, and modeling power for systems where physical correctness matters.
sentences.zh: MuJoCo 强调物理正确性很重要的系统的速度、准确性和建模能力。

blocks.html_en: While MuJoCo is not always the best choice for photorealistic rendering or massive GPU-parallel simulation, but some of the core features that MuJoCo is well known for are a deterministic pipeline, well-defined inverse dynamics with contacts, strong contact modeling, and generalized-coordinate simulation.

blocks.html_en: MuJoCo Warp

sentences.en: MuJoCo Warp
sentences.zh: MuJoCo 扭曲

blocks.html_en: MuJoCo Warp, also called MJWarp, is a GPU-accelerated implementation of MuJoCo written in NVIDIA Warp, a Python framework for writing high-performance CUDA-accelerated differentiable kernels - tailored for robot learning and physics simulation.

sentences.en: MuJoCo Warp, also called MJWarp, is a GPU-accelerated implementation of MuJoCo written in NVIDIA Warp, a Python framework for writing high-performance CUDA-accelerated differentiable kernels - tailored for robot learning and physics simulation.
sentences.zh: MuJoCo Warp 也称为 MJWarp，是用 NVIDIA Warp 编写的 MuJoCo 的 GPU 加速实现，NVIDIA Warp 是一个 Python 框架，用于编写高性能 CUDA 加速的可微内核 - 专为机器人学习和物理模拟而定制。

blocks.html_en: It brings MuJoCo-style articulated-body physics, contact-rich simulation, and robot dynamics into a batched GPU setting, making it well suited for reinforcement learning and large-scale policy training. Compared with classic CPU MuJoCo, MJWarp is aimed more at throughput than single-step latency: it is designed to simulate many worlds in parallel, reduce CPU-GPU transfer bottlenecks, and scale contact-heavy robot tasks more efficiently for learning workloads.

sentences.en: It brings MuJoCo-style articulated-body physics, contact-rich simulation, and robot dynamics into a batched GPU setting, making it well suited for reinforcement learning and large-scale policy training.
sentences.zh: 它将 MuJoCo 式的关节体物理、接触丰富的模拟和机器人动力学引入批量 GPU 设置中，使其非常适合强化学习和大规模策略训练。

sentences.en: Compared with classic CPU MuJoCo, MJWarp is aimed more at throughput than single-step latency: it is designed to simulate many worlds in parallel, reduce CPU-GPU transfer bottlenecks, and scale contact-heavy robot tasks more efficiently for learning workloads.
sentences.zh: 与经典的 CPU MuJoCo 相比，MJWarp 更注重吞吐量而不是单步延迟：它旨在并行模拟多个世界，减少 CPU-GPU 传输瓶颈，并更有效地扩展接触密集型机器人任务以实现学习工作负载。

blocks.html_en: Isaac Sim

sentences.en: Isaac Sim
sentences.zh: 艾萨克·辛

blocks.html_en: Isaac Sim is an open-source robotics simulation framework built on NVIDIA Omniverse. It uses OpenUSD as its core scene and data layer, allowing robots, environments, sensors, materials, lighting, and physics properties to be represented as USD prims and schemas. Isaac Sim provides high-fidelity physics through PhysX, photorealistic RTX rendering, and robotics-focused sensor simulation for cameras, depth, lidar, radar, segmentation, and synthetic data generation workflows. It can ingest assets from CAD, URDF, MJCF, USD, and real-world reconstruction pipelines, enabling robots to be tested in realistic digital-twin environments.

blocks.html_en: NVIDIA Isaac Lab 3.0 is an open-source, GPU-accelerated, agent-ready simulation framework for robot learning, designed to train and evaluate robot policies at scale. This direction supports agent-assisted workflows for building environments, tuning physics, debugging, profiling, sim-to-sim transfer, and sim-to-real deployment. Its modular manager-based and direct workflows support reinforcement learning, imitation learning, motion planning, data collection, and policy evaluation.

blocks.html_en: With Isaac Lab 3.0.0 - We have decoupled the Isaac Sim and omniverse dependency, and made Isaac Lab a lightweight, multi backend Robot learning framework.

sentences.en: With Isaac Lab 3.0.0 - We have decoupled the Isaac Sim and omniverse dependency, and made Isaac Lab a lightweight, multi backend Robot learning framework.
sentences.zh: 通过 Isaac Lab 3.0.0 - 我们解耦了 Isaac Sim 和全维依赖关系，并使 Isaac Lab 成为一个轻量级、多后端的机器人学习框架。

blocks.html_en: The new architecture separates backend-specific code from the core Isaac Lab API. Developers can select to use Isaac Sim with PhysX and RTX rendering for photorealistic, sensor-rich workflows, or run light-weightheadless Newton physics for high-throughput simulation. Additionally, developers can add photoreal sensors via the standalone OVRTX renderer, or leverage the Newton renderer for vision-based RL tasks that require a very large number of environments.

blocks.html_en: Modern GPU-Accelerated Physics for Robotics

sentences.en: Modern GPU-Accelerated Physics for Robotics
sentences.zh: 现代 GPU 加速机器人物理

blocks.html_en: Modern robotics simulation frameworks are powered by numerical physics: rigid-body and soft-body dynamics, contact and friction models, joint constraints, actuators, sensors, and time integration. At each simulation step, the engine estimates how bodies move under forces, torques, collisions, gravity, constraints, and controller inputs. For robot learning, this physics must be fast, stable, repeatable, and scalable enough to run thousands of parallel environments, while also being accurate enough to transfer learned behavior from simulation to real robots. To address these, the Newton library is created.

sentences.en: Modern robotics simulation frameworks are powered by numerical physics: rigid-body and soft-body dynamics, contact and friction models, joint constraints, actuators, sensors, and time integration.
sentences.zh: 现代机器人仿真框架由数值物理学提供支持：刚体和软体动力学、接触和摩擦模型、关节约束、执行器、传感器和时间积分。

blocks.html_en: Newton

sentences.en: Newton
sentences.zh: 牛顿

blocks.html_en: Newton is an open-source, GPU-accelerated, extensible, and differentiable physics engine developed by NVIDIA, Google DeepMind, and Disney Research, and managed through the Linux Foundation. It is built on NVIDIA Warp and OpenUSD, and it integrates MuJoCo Warp as a key physics backend. In the broader simulation ecosystem, Newton acts as a modern physics layer for robot learning frameworks such as Isaac Lab and MuJoCo Playground, giving researchers a path toward scalable, differentiable, simulator-agnostic physics. In Isaac Lab specifically, Newton is currently an active integration path, with the MuJoCo-Warp solver used as a primary focus for high-throughput robot-learning experiments. Figure showcases breakdown on how different application frameworks communicate with GPU accelerated physics libraries and simulation engines. Newton provides multiple solver implementations rather than prescribing one numerical method for every problem. SolverMuJoCo and SolverFeatherstone use generalized coordinates for articulated rigid-body systems. SolverSemiImplicit, SolverXPBD, and the SolverKamino use maximal-coordinate formulations. SolverVBD is an implicit solver supporting rigid bodies, particles, cloth, and soft bodies, although its joint support remains limited. SolverImplicitMPM targets particle-based continuum materials, while SolverStyle3D specializes in cloth simulation. Because capabilities differ between solvers—including support for articulations, deformable bodies, contacts, and differentiation—the appropriate solver depends on the physical system being modeled.

sentences.en: Newton is an open-source, GPU-accelerated, extensible, and differentiable physics engine developed by NVIDIA, Google DeepMind, and Disney Research, and managed through the Linux Foundation.
sentences.zh: Newton 是一款开源、GPU 加速、可扩展且可微分的物理引擎，由 NVIDIA、Google DeepMind 和 Disney Research 开发，并通过 Linux 基金会进行管理。

blocks.html_en: Conclusion

sentences.en: Conclusion

blocks.html_en: Robot simulation has quietly become one of the most important layers in the modern embodied AI stack. As models move from static datasets into physical interaction, the ability to generate high-quality, scalable, and diverse simulated experiences is no longer optional, it is foundational. Simulation is where policies are stress-tested, where data is amplified, and increasingly, where entire training pipelines begin.

sentences.zh: 模拟是对政策进行压力测试的地方，是数据被放大的地方，而且越来越多地是整个培训渠道的开始处。

blocks.html_en: For a long time, the robot-simulator conversation has focused on "which engine performs the fastest" and for many workloads this statement still holds true. What we find more interesting in 2026 is a different question: which pieces of the stack are likely to become the shared infrastructure that different engines would build on top of? There is no definite answer out there yet but a few candidates seem plausible from where we sit. The ecosystem is fragmenting and recomposing into a layered stack, where different tools specialize, interoperate, and sometimes compete to define the abstractions others will build on.

That alone is worth celebrating, regardless of which engines you end up reaching for.

content_html_zh:
返回文章

为什么要进行仿真 我应该使用哪个仿真引擎？ MuJoCo MuJoCo Warp Isaac Sim Isaac Lab Isaac Lab 3.0 与 Isaac Sim 和 Newton 有何关系 用于机器人的现代 GPU 加速物理学 Newton 其他仿真引擎 结论 为什么要仿真

图 1：人形机器人运动模拟。 机器人的姿势由跟踪的身体关键点（绿色标记）表示，而连续的机器人实例则说明其随时间的运动。 方向箭头指示命令运动，演示了如何使用基于物理的模拟环境来训练和评估机器人运动和控制策略。

构建物理人工智能系统的主要挑战是数据可用性。 大型语言模型（LLM）和视觉语言模型（VLM）可以在互联网规模的数据集上进行训练，但机器人和物理人工智能系统不具备同样的优势。 为了训练物理人工智能系统，机器人必须了解与物理世界交互的后果。 例如，它需要了解当杯子滑动、电缆弯曲或夹具以错误角度接触物体时会发生什么。

在现实世界中收集此类数据缓慢、昂贵、有风险，而且由于任务的破坏性，有时是不切实际的。 模拟提供了一座桥梁，使开发人员能够生成大量逼真的、基于物理的数据。 通过在模拟中远程操作机器人并通过 GPU 并行性扩展数据收集，开发人员可以以实际收集成本的一小部分来生成数千小时的机器人体验。

早期的机器人模拟器通常主要用于调试几何结构、测试控制器或可视化机器人运动。 如今，仿真已成为模型开发循环的一部分。 团队使用它来生成感知数据集、训练强化学习策略、收集演示、增强现实世界数据、基准模型以及针对罕见或对抗场景测试策略。 这种转变就是工业研究实验室和学术团体越来越多地致力于或开发能够满足这些新要求的模拟引擎的原因。

这些要求可以通过三计算机范例来理解：

  • 训练计算机：用于处理数据和训练基础人工智能模型的大型 GPU 集群。
  • 模拟计算机：GPU 工作站或集群，使用 GPU 加速物理和 RTX 渲染来生成机器人体验、传感器数据和模拟交互。
  • 机器人计算机：边缘设备，例如 NVIDIA Jetson AGX Thor 级系统，在部署期间运行经过训练的策略或模型。

图 2：物理系统（地球和机器人）与其虚拟表示（数字模型）不断交换数据，从而通过双向反馈回路实现监视、分析、预测和控制。

每台计算机根据任务的延迟、吞吐量、准确性和部署要求发挥不同的作用。

如今有许多仿真引擎可供使用，每个引擎都针对不同的用例，例如人形机器人、飞行器、地面机器人、自动驾驶车辆、四足动物和灵巧机械手。 这些引擎的不同之处还在于对强化学习、批量模拟、接触丰富的物理、真实感渲染和传感器模拟的支持。 请参阅图 2，了解按机器人领域和加速曲线对仿真引擎进行的分类。

我应该使用哪个仿真引擎？

开发人员经常纠结于要使用哪种模拟引擎来处理他们正在处理的用例。 通常，这取决于一些基本问题，例如

  • 我是否需要可扩展的合成数据生成工作流程？
  • 我需要进行强化学习吗？
  • 给定的模拟引擎有什么样的传感器支持？
  • 模拟引擎支持哪些3D资源格式？
  • 需要什么样的环境保真度和规模？

这些因素将帮助开发人员决定使用哪个模拟引擎来满足他们的开发需求。

NVIDIA Isaac Sim 和 Isaac Lab、MuJoCo、PyBullet、Drake 和 Genesis 是最流行的人形和两臂灵巧操作模拟引擎。 这些模拟引擎中的每一个对于特定用例都是独特的，例如扩展强化学习、生成合成数据、在模拟中测试算法和/或模型。 在这里，我们将快速概述 MuJoCo、MuJoCo Warp、NVIDIA Isaac Sim 和 Isaac Lab。

穆乔科

MuJoCo 是“接触式多关节动力学”的缩写，是一种快速、准确、开源的物理引擎，主要用于机器人、生物力学、强化学习、控制和关节系统的仿真。 与主要关注游戏或视觉真实感的引擎不同，MuJoCo 是围绕精确的动力学、丰富的接触运动和基于模型的优化而设计的。 MuJoCo 强调物理正确性很重要的系统的速度、准确性和建模能力。

图 3：在 MuJoCo 中模拟的机器人操纵器。 该图展示了在基于物理的模拟环境中运行的带有平行夹具的多关节机械臂。 MuJoCo 用于对机器人的动力学、运动学和接触交互进行建模，从而能够在实际部署之前开发和评估运动规划、操纵和控制算法。

虽然 MuJoCo 并不总是真实感渲染或大规模 GPU 并行模拟的最佳选择，但 MuJoCo 众所周知的一些核心功能是确定性管道、明确定义的接触逆动力学、强接触建模和广义坐标模拟。

MuJoCo 扭曲

MuJoCo Warp 也称为 MJWarp，是用 NVIDIA Warp 编写的 MuJoCo 的 GPU 加速实现，NVIDIA Warp 是一个 Python 框架，用于编写高性能 CUDA 加速的可微内核 - 专为机器人学习和物理模拟而定制。

它将 MuJoCo 式的关节体物理、接触丰富的模拟和机器人动力学引入批量 GPU 设置中，使其非常适合强化学习和大规模策略训练。 与经典的 CPU MuJoCo 相比，MJWarp 更注重吞吐量而不是单步延迟：它旨在并行模拟多个世界，减少 CPU-GPU 传输瓶颈，并更有效地扩展接触密集型机器人任务以实现学习工作负载。

艾萨克·辛

Isaac Sim 是一个基于 NVIDIA Omniverse 构建的开源机器人模拟框架。 它使用 OpenUSD 作为其核心场景和数据层，允许机器人、环境、传感器、材料、照明和物理属性表示为 USD prims 和 schema。 Isaac Sim 通过 PhysX、真实感 RTX 渲染和针对相机、深度、激光雷达、雷达、分割和合成数据生成工作流程的以机器人为中心的传感器模拟提供高保真物理。 它可以从 CAD、URDF、MJCF、USD 和现实世界的重建管道中获取资产，使机器人能够在现实的数字孪生环境中进行测试。

艾萨克实验室

NVIDIA Isaac Lab 3.0 是一个开源、GPU 加速、代理就绪的机器人学习模拟框架，旨在大规模训练和评估机器人策略。 该方向支持代理辅助工作流程，用于构建环境、调整物理、调试、分析、模拟到模拟传输以及模拟到真实部署。 其基于模块化管理器的直接工作流程支持强化学习、模仿学习、运动规划、数据收集和策略评估。

Isaac Lab 3.0 与 Isaac Sim 和 Newton 有何关系

通过 Isaac Lab 3.0.0 - 我们解耦了 Isaac Sim 和全维依赖关系，并使 Isaac Lab 成为一个轻量级、多后端的机器人学习框架。

新架构将后端特定代码与核心 Isaac Lab API 分开。 开发人员可以选择将 Isaac Sim 与 PhysX 和 RTX 渲染结合使用，以实现逼真、传感器丰富的工作流程，或运行轻量级无头牛顿物理学以进行高吞吐量模拟。 此外，开发人员可以通过独立的 OVRTX 渲染器添加照片级传感器，或者利用 Newton 渲染器来执行需要大量环境的基于视觉的 RL 任务。

现代 GPU 加速机器人物理

现代机器人仿真框架由数值物理学提供支持：刚体和软体动力学、接触和摩擦模型、关节约束、执行器、传感器和时间积分。 在每个模拟步骤中，引擎都会估计物体在力、扭矩、碰撞、重力、约束和控制器输入的作用下如何移动。 对于机器人学习来说，这种物理现象必须足够快速、稳定、可重复和可扩展，以运行数千个并行环境，同时也足够准确，以便将学习到的行为从模拟转移到真实的机器人。 为了解决这些问题，创建了牛顿库。

牛顿

Newton 是一款开源、GPU 加速、可扩展且可微分的物理引擎，由 NVIDIA、Google DeepMind 和 Disney Research 开发，并通过 Linux 基金会进行管理。 它基于 NVIDIA Warp 和 OpenUSD 构建，并集成 MuJoCo Warp 作为关键物理后端。 在更广泛的模拟生态系统中，Newton 充当 Isaac Lab 和 MuJoCo Playground 等机器人学习框架的现代物理层，为研究人员提供了一条通往可扩展、可微分、与模拟器无关的物理的道路。 具体来说，在 Isaac Lab 中，Newton 目前是一条积极的集成路径，MuJoCo-Warp 求解器用作高通量机器人学习实验的主要焦点。 该图展示了不同应用程序框架如何与 GPU 加速物理库和模拟引擎进行通信的详细信息。 牛顿提供了多种求解器实现，而不是为每个问题指定一种数值方法。 SolverMuJoCo 和 SolverFeatherstone 对铰接刚体系统使用广义坐标。 SolverSemiImplicit、SolverXPBD 和 SolverKamino 使用最大坐标公式。 SolverVBD 是一个隐式解算器，支持刚体、粒子、布料和软体，但其关节支持仍然有限。 SolverImplicitMPM 针对的是基于粒子的连续材料，而 SolverStyle3D 则专注于布料模拟。 由于解算器之间的功能不同（包括对关节、变形体、接触和微分的支持），合适的解算器取决于所建模的物理系统。

图 4：Newton 物理引擎是一款基于 NVIDIA Warp 构建的开源 GPU 加速模拟引擎，专为机器人专家和模拟研究人员而设计。

其他模拟引擎

PyBullet 仍然是快速原型设计的有用 CPU 基准； DART 和 ODE 仍然是 Gazebo 后端；如果您需要接触隐式轨迹优化和严格的数值而不是吞吐量，Drake 是黄金标准。 每个都有一个利基。 它们都无法回答“我需要在一个 GPU 上运行 4,096 个类人机器人”的问题。

结论

机器人模拟已悄然成为现代人工智能堆栈中最重要的层之一。 随着模型从静态数据集转向物理交互，生成高质量、可扩展且多样化的模拟体验的能力不再是可选的，而是基础。 模拟是对政策进行压力测试的地方，是数据被放大的地方，而且越来越多地是整个培训渠道的开始处。

长期以来，机器人模拟器的对话一直集中在“哪个引擎执行速度最快”，对于许多工作负载来说，这一说法仍然适用。 我们发现 2026 年更有趣的是一个不同的问题：堆栈的哪些部分可能成为不同引擎构建的共享基础设施？ 目前还没有明确的答案，但从我们的角度来看，一些候选人似乎是合理的。 生态系统正在分裂并重新组合成一个分层堆栈，其中不同的工具专门化、互操作，有时甚至竞争定义其他工具将构建的抽象。

我们确实有信心地说，大量的工作是在开源、公开管理的情况下完成的，并且任何拥有消费级 GPU 的人都可以使用。 越来越多的核心基础设施物理引擎、可微分模拟器和训练管道以开放方式出现，开源项目、开放治理和 GPU 可访问工具降低了进入门槛并加速了迭代。 无论您最终选择哪种引擎，仅这一点就值得庆祝。

展望未来：从概念到实践

本文回顾了 2026 年的情况：存在什么、主要引擎如何相互关联，以及为什么这些转变对任何构建物理 AI 的人都很重要。 本系列的下一篇文章将深入探讨 Warp 和 MuJoCo Warp 本身、它是什么以及如何驱动它。 从这里开始，我们将继续努力，到本系列结束时，我们将拥有一个实用的端到端机器人示例，该示例使用两年前不存在的软件堆栈在 GPU 上进行训练。
